items.myth: Universal approximation means any MLP will learn any function from finite data.
items.reality: UAT is an existence theorem about representation with enough hidden units and correctly chosen weights. Learning from finite noisy samples, optimization, and generalization are separate challenges.
items.example: A 50-unit net on 30 noisy points may memorize labels (zero train error) yet fail on validation — UAT compatible, generalization not guaranteed.

items.myth: You always need many hidden layers because shallow networks cannot approximate complex functions.
items.reality: Classic UAT: one sufficiently wide hidden layer suffices for continuous functions on compact sets. Depth can be more parameter-efficient for some functions but is not required by the basic theorem.
items.example: A single hidden layer with 512 sigmoid units can approximate a smooth 2-D surface on [0,1]² — depth adds efficiency, not basic possibility.

items.myth: If the network is big enough, overfitting is impossible.
items.reality: Large expressive networks are more prone to fitting noise when data is limited — the opposite of guaranteed generalization. Capacity from UAT must be balanced with data and regularization.
items.example: 10k hidden units on 200 rows → near-zero training loss and poor validation AUC — textbook overfitting despite expressivity.

items.myth: ReLU networks are not covered by universal approximation.
items.reality: Modern variants of UAT extend to ReLU and other activations under standard conditions on compact domains. ReLU MLPs are universal approximators in the same broad sense as sigmoid nets.
items.example: nn.Linear → nn.ReLU → nn.Linear stacks are the default universal approximator class in modern deep learning practice.
